What metadata actually matters when I upload a release? I usually just fill in title, artist name, and genre. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Metadata is the invisible engine behind discoverability. DSP algorithms use it to decide who hears your music.

Essential: Track title (clean formatting, no ALL CAPS). Contributing artists (full names for all featured/primary). ISRC code (unique per recording, never reuse across versions). UPC/EAN (release level). Genre AND sub-genre (be specific — 'Hip-Hop/Lo-fi Hip Hop' beats just 'Hip-Hop'). Release date (set 3-4 weeks out for playlist pitching). Copyright info.

Critical for Discovery: Mood tags (Happy, Melancholic, Energetic, Chill — DSPs use these for mood playlists). BPM (workout playlists, DJ tools). Key (mood algorithms). Lyrics (full lyrics improve search on Apple Music and Spotify). Songwriter/Producer credits (feeds PRO matching). Language. Explicit flag (accurate labeling prevents editorial playlist exclusion).

Power Moves: ISNI/IPI numbers to link identity across databases. Pre-save links via Feature.fm.

Common mistakes: Listing producer as 'featured artist' instead of in credits. Different artist name spellings across releases (fragments your profile). Wrong auto-detected BPM. Incorrect territory rights.

Treat metadata as part of release prep — same importance as mixing and mastering.

My QC system flagged one of my submitted tracks as potentially being an MP3 converted to WAV (lossy-to-lossless fraud). Spotify rejected it with error 'audio quality does not meet minimum standards.' I believe the original is genuine 24-bit/48kHz. Walk me through the forensic tests I should run to confirm or deny, and what evidence I need for the DSP appeal. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":false,"hasImages":false}]

MP3-to-WAV conversion creates a characteristic spectral signature that's detectable in under 5 minutes. Here's the forensic protocol: **Test 1 — Spectral analysis (Adobe Audition or Audacity):** Open the WAV in spectral view. A genuine 24-bit/48kHz file should show full frequency content up to 24kHz (Nyquist). An MP3-converted file shows a hard brick wall cutoff at 16kHz (for 128kbps MP3), 18kHz (192kbps), or 20kHz (320kbps) — above that cutoff, energy drops to noise floor instantly. If you see this pattern: converted file. **Test 2 — Histogram analysis:** Check the bit depth histogram. A true 24-bit file has bit utilization across all 24 bits. An MP3 converted to 24-bit will show a characteristic 'staircase' pattern in the lower 8 bits — the lossy compression rounding is visible. **Test 3 — Phase coherence:** MP3 encoding alters stereo phase relationships. Run a mid-side analysis (Tools > Stereo Field in Audition) — lossy-encoded files show mid-side inconsistencies that genuine 24-bit recordings don't. **If the file is genuine:** Export a timestamped screenshot of each test showing clean results. For the Spotify appeal, file through your distributor with: (1) DAW session export showing original 24-bit/48kHz settings, (2) spectral screenshot showing clean 24kHz extension, (3) bit depth histogram, (4) original session bounce file metadata. DSPs process appeals in 5–10 business days.

Is Bandcamp still worth using? Everyone says streaming is where it's at but I see some indie artists doing well on Bandcamp. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Bandcamp is not just 'worth using' — for many indie artists it's the HIGHEST revenue-per-fan platform available. Here's why:

The revenue math comparison: Spotify: $0.003-$0.005 per stream. A fan who plays your 10-track album 10 times = 100 streams = ~$0.40. Bandcamp: a fan who buys your album for $10 on Bandcamp = you keep $8.50 (Bandcamp takes 15% on digital, 10% on physical). To earn $8.50 from one fan on Spotify, that fan would need to stream your music approximately 2,125 times. Translation: ONE Bandcamp sale = 2,125 Spotify streams in revenue.

When Bandcamp excels: (1) Album releases and pre-orders. (2) Limited edition physical releases (vinyl, cassette, special packaging). (3) Merchandise bundled with music. (4) Name-your-price releases (fans often pay above the minimum). (5) Genres with collector-oriented fans: indie rock, electronic, ambient, metal, jazz, experimental.

Bandcamp Friday: On the first Friday of every month, Bandcamp waives their revenue share — you keep 100% of digital sales (minus payment processing ~3-5%). This is a massive marketing opportunity. Promote aggressively on these days.

Bandcamp strategy: (1) Price your digital album at $7-$10 with 'name your price' enabled. Fans often pay $12-$15. (2) Offer exclusive formats: bonus tracks only on Bandcamp, early access, limited merch bundles. (3) Use Bandcamp's built-in email list — message your followers directly about new releases. (4) Enable streaming on Bandcamp — fans can listen before buying. (5) Physical + digital bundles convert well: vinyl + digital download at $30-$40.

Bandcamp vs streaming — it's not either/or: Use streaming (Spotify, Apple Music) for DISCOVERY — new fans find you here. Use Bandcamp for CONVERSION — once a fan is engaged, direct them to Bandcamp for purchases. Your social media link-in-bio should include BOTH your Spotify and your Bandcamp. For new releases: push Bandcamp in the first week (capture high-intent fans), then shift promotion to streaming for algorithmic discovery.

Revenue potential: An artist with 1,000 true fans buying one $10 release per year on Bandcamp = $8,500/year from Bandcamp alone. Add merch bundles, physical releases, and one-off digital singles, and $15,000-$25,000/year from Bandcamp is realistic for a moderately successful indie artist.

How does YouTube Music work with regular YouTube? Do my music video views count as streams? How do I optimize for both? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Theo","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":150000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Theo, YouTube has one of the most complex ecosystems in music distribution because it bridges video AND audio. Here is how it works.

Types of YouTube Music Content:

1. Art Tracks (Auto-Generated): When your distributor delivers to YouTube Music, an 'Art Track' is automatically created. This is a static image (your cover art) with your audio playing. It lives on the 'Topic' channel (e.g., 'Theo - Topic'). These are the equivalent of a Spotify stream.

2. Official Music Videos: Videos you upload to YOUR YouTube channel. YouTube Music can link these to your Art Track so listeners can switch between the audio-only and video version.

3. User-Generated Content (UGC): Videos OTHER people upload using your music. Content ID catches these and either: monetizes them (you earn revenue), tracks them, or blocks them.

How Views/Streams Count:
- YouTube Music streams of Art Tracks: Counted as premium streams, higher per-stream rate ($0.004-$0.007)
- YouTube Music streams from music videos: Also counted as premium streams
- Regular YouTube views of music videos: Counted as ad-supported views, lower rate ($0.001-$0.003)
- UGC with your music (Content ID): Variable rate based on the video's ad revenue

Optimization Strategy:

1. Claim Your Official Artist Channel: Go to YouTube.com/artists. Link your Topic channel to your official channel. This merges your Art Tracks and your uploaded videos into one unified artist profile.

2. Content ID Registration: Ensure your distributor has registered ALL your music with YouTube Content ID. This automatically monetizes any user-generated content that uses your audio.

3. YouTube Shorts: Short-form content (under 60 seconds) drives massive discovery. Create YouTube Shorts featuring snippets of your music. Shorts using your audio create a similar viral loop to TikTok.

4. Official Music Videos: Upload in the highest quality possible (4K if available). Add proper metadata: song title, artist, album in the title. Use cards and end screens.

5. Monetization: Apply for the YouTube Partner Program (1,000 subscribers + 4,000 watch hours). This unlocks ad revenue on your uploads beyond just Content ID.

I want to join Merlin to get premium DSP rates. I have 200 tracks across 2 labels generating $95k in annual streaming revenue through DistroKid. Do I qualify directly, or do I need to go through a Merlin member aggregator? What's the actual premium I'd receive and how do I calculate the uplift? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":false,"hasImages":false}]

Merlin direct membership requires demonstrating that you are a 'rights holder' — meaning you own or exclusively control the masters you're licensing. The revenue threshold for direct membership is not publicly published, but Merlin's intake process requires you to be a label or distributor (not an artist using a retail aggregator). DistroKid is a retail aggregator, not a label — this is a critical distinction. **Your path to Merlin benefits:** You have two options: (1) Direct membership: Incorporate your two labels as proper entities, demonstrate ownership of all 200 masters, and apply at merlinnetwork.org. Revenue of $95k/year is at the lower end — Merlin's membership has trended toward larger catalogs. (2) Merlin aggregator route: Many Merlin members offer label services that pass Merlin rate premiums downstream. Examples: AWAL (Kobalt), The Orchard, CD Baby Pro, or DistroKid's 'Label' tier which licenses through a Merlin member. Ask DistroKid if their Label tier passes Merlin rates. **The premium:** Merlin has negotiated rates approximately 15–25% above non-member rates at Spotify and Apple Music. On $95k annual streaming: 15% premium = ~$14,250/year additional income. At 25%: ~$23,750/year. The uplift is real and meaningful at your revenue level. Given your catalog size, the aggregator route (option 2) is faster and more practical than direct membership.

I'm a UK resident individual musician receiving royalties from my US distributor. They're withholding 30% federal tax. I've been told the US-UK treaty provides a 0% rate on royalties. What form do I file, exactly what do I write in each relevant field, and how long does it take for withholding to stop? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":false,"hasImages":false}]

You're being over-withheld. The US-UK Double Taxation Convention, Article 12(1) provides a 0% withholding rate on royalties paid to UK beneficial owners — no withholding at all. File Form W-8BEN with your distributor: **Line 1:** Your full legal name. **Line 2:** Country of citizenship: United Kingdom. **Line 3:** Leave blank (individual, not entity). **Line 5:** Your UK address. **Line 6:** Leave blank (no US TIN required for treaty claim at 0%). **Line 9 (Part II — Treaty Claim):** Country: United Kingdom. **Line 10:** Article: 12. Paragraph: 1. Rate: 0%. Type of income: Royalties. Reason: I am a resident of the United Kingdom as defined in the treaty and qualify as the beneficial owner of these royalties. **Signature and date.** Submit to your distributor's compliance team. W-8BEN is valid for 3 years. **Timeline:** Most US distributors update withholding within 30–45 days of receiving a valid W-8BEN. They cannot retroactively refund over-withheld amounts — but you can claim those as a foreign tax credit on your UK self-assessment return, or file a US Form 1040-NR to claim a refund of excess withholding. File the W-8BEN today to stop future over-withholding.

I made a typo in the track title. Can I fix it after release? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Yes — calling update_track_metadata. The corrected metadata will be submitted to DSPs via an ERN 4.3 update message. Propagation time: 48-72 hours for Spotify, up to 7 days for Apple Music. Note: The ISRC stays the same — ISRCs are permanent. Only metadata is updated. Large changes (artist name, release date) may trigger re-review at some DSPs.

We licensed a track that can only be released in North America — the publishing rights in Europe are held by a different company. I need to block it from all EU countries, UK, Switzerland, and Norway. How do I set this up in distribution? And what happens if a European user finds it on Spotify via VPN? Are we liable if someone in Germany streams it through a US VPN? [Context: {"territory_restriction":"North America only","excluded_territories":["EU","UK","Switzerland","Norway"],"included_territories":["US","Canada","Mexico"],"concern":"VPN circumvention liability"}]

Here's how to configure territory restrictions correctly and what it means for VPN access:

**Territory Setup in DDEX (ERN field):**
In your DDEX delivery, the territory restriction goes in the `TerritoryCode` field of the `DealTerms` section:
```xml
<TerritoryCode>US</TerritoryCode>
<TerritoryCode>CA</TerritoryCode>
<TerritoryCode>MX</TerritoryCode>
<!-- Or use: ExcludedTerritoryCode for the exclusion approach -->
<ExcludedTerritoryCode>GB</ExcludedTerritoryCode>
<ExcludedTerritoryCode>DE</ExcludedTerritoryCode>
<!-- ... all EU member states + NO + CH -->
```
Most aggregators let you set this in their UI. Select 'Worldwide except' and then check the territories to exclude — or select only US/CA/MX explicitly.

**ISO codes for your exclusion list:**
EU (27 member states) + GB + CH + NO + IS + LI (EEA non-EU members) = 31 territories to block.

**What Happens with VPN:**
Spotify and Apple Music detect the user's **billing country** and **IP address** separately. If a German user has a German payment account, Spotify will serve them German geo-restrictions even if their VPN IP appears US-based. Spotify's terms prohibit VPN circumvention. In practice:
- Most geo-blocking is effective for casual VPN use
- Power users with US billing accounts + VPN CAN slip through — but this is a tiny edge case

**Your Liability:**
Your obligation under the licensing agreement is to implement reasonable technical measures — which DSP geo-blocking satisfies. You are **not** liable for individual user circumvention via VPN; that liability sits with the user who violates the DSP's terms of service. Your licensor can only hold you liable if you fail to configure the restriction at all.

**Verification:**
After delivery, verify in Spotify for Artists → Music → select the track → check availability — it should show only US/CA/MX flags. For Apple, check App Store Connect → My Music → territory availability. Check within 72 hours of release to confirm propagation.

**Important**: If you also have a music video or lyric video on YouTube, you must set the same geo-restriction separately in YouTube Studio → Content → Restrict — DDEX delivery does not automatically propagate to YouTube.

My song suddenly got 50,000 streams overnight from a country I've never promoted in. Spotify said they detected artificial streaming. I didn't do anything! What's happening? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

This is unfortunately common and it's NOT always the artist's fault. Here's what's likely happening and how to fix it:

What causes artificial streaming you didn't buy: (1) Your song was added to a fraudulent playlist. Bot operators create large playlists with real songs to generate fake streams. They do this to inflate their own playlist follower counts or to sell 'playlist placement' services to other artists. Your song is caught in the crossfire. (2) A 'fan' purchased streams for you without your knowledge. (3) A competitor is intentionally triggering artificial streaming flags to get your music removed (rare but documented).

What Spotify does: Spotify's anti-fraud system detects unnatural listening patterns (same IP ranges, identical play durations, geographic concentration from regions with no organic listeners). They remove the artificial streams from your count. If the behavior continues, they may issue a warning, withhold royalties for the affected streams, or in extreme cases, remove the track entirely.

How to respond: (1) DON'T PANIC. Contact your distributor immediately and explain that you did not purchase or solicit these streams. (2) File a dispute through Spotify for Artists. Use the 'Contact Support' option and explain the situation with specifics (date of the spike, country, that you have no marketing activity in that region). (3) Check your Spotify for Artists analytics: look at the 'Source of streams' section. If the streams are from a specific playlist you don't recognize, report that playlist to Spotify. (4) Document everything — screenshots of your analytics showing the anomaly.

Prevention: Regularly monitor your Spotify for Artists for unusual spikes. Never purchase streams from any service — Spotify's detection is sophisticated. Never accept 'free promotion' from unknown accounts offering playlist placement. If you find your song on suspicious playlists, report them.

If Spotify removes your track: You have the right to appeal. Provide evidence that you didn't engage in artificial streaming. Include your marketing timeline showing no paid promotion to the flagged region. Most legitimate cases are resolved in the artist's favor within 2-4 weeks.

I keep hearing about 'user-centric payouts' and how Spotify might switch to them. What does that mean for indie artists? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

This is one of the most important structural changes in music economics. Here's how the two models work and what they mean for you:

The Current Model — Pro-Rata (Big Pool): How it works: ALL subscription revenue goes into one giant pool. Payouts are distributed based on total platform-wide stream share. Example: Spotify collects $1 billion in subscription revenue this month. Drake accounts for 3% of all streams. Drake gets $30 million. You account for 0.00001% of all streams. You get $100. The problem: if a subscriber ONLY listens to YOUR music all month but Drake gets 3% of total streams, your subscriber's $10.99 still flows mostly to Drake. Heavy listeners of mainstream pop effectively subsidize major label revenue.

The Alternative Model — User-Centric Payout (UCPS): How it works: each subscriber's $10.99 is distributed only to the artists THEY actually listened to. Example: Subscriber pays $10.99. They listen to 100 songs this month: 60 of your songs, 30 from Artist B, 10 from Artist C. After Spotify's cut (~30%): ~$7.69 distributed. You get: 60% × $7.69 = $4.61. Artist B gets: 30% × $7.69 = $2.31. Artist C gets: 10% × $7.69 = $0.77.

Impact on indie artists: UCPS significantly benefits: niche artists with dedicated fan bases (fans who play your music heavily), genres with loyal listeners (jazz, classical, indie, folk), and artists whose fans pay for subscriptions (vs. free-tier listeners). UCPS hurts: background/ambient playlists that accumulate billions of passive streams, artists whose streams come from casual listeners, and gaming/noise/white noise channels.

Where we are in 2026: Deezer implemented user-centric payouts in 2025-2026 — they call it the 'Artist-Centric Model.' Tidal has experimented with direct-to-artist payouts. SoundCloud has 'Fan-Powered Royalties' (their version of UCPS). Spotify has NOT switched but has implemented a 'minimum stream threshold' (1,000 streams/year to earn royalties) — a partial step toward filtering low-engagement content.

What you should do: (1) Build deep engagement, not wide reach. Under UCPS, 100 fans who each stream your album 50 times is worth more than 5,000 fans who each stream one song once. (2) Encourage subscription conversion — fans on paid plans contribute more revenue per stream. (3) Distribute to Deezer, Tidal, and SoundCloud where you may already benefit from artist-centric models. (4) Build direct-to-fan revenue (Bandcamp, Patreon) that doesn't depend on ANY payout model.

I feel like Spotify's algorithm gave up on me. I had one song that did well 2 years ago but nothing since. How do I reset or rebuild my algorithmic standing? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Spotify's algorithm doesn't 'give up' on artists — but it does deprioritize you if the signals weaken. Here's what's happening and how to rebuild:

How Spotify's algorithm evaluates you: Spotify builds a 'listener cohort' profile for every artist — the types of listeners who engage with your music (demographics, listening habits, taste clusters). When you release new music, the algorithm uses this cohort to determine who to serve it to. If your previous releases performed well (high save rate, low skip rate, good completion rate), the algorithm serves your new music to a larger pool. If they underperformed, the pool shrinks.

Why your momentum stalled: (1) Release gap: if you went 6+ months without releasing, your listener cohort has moved on and Spotify deprioritized your profile. (2) Mismatched releases: if your hit was pop but you released experimental ambient music next, the algorithm serves it to your pop cohort — they skip it — and the algorithm interprets that as poor quality. (3) Low save rate on recent releases: your existing fans aren't saving, which signals to the algorithm that even your core audience isn't excited.

The rebuild strategy: (1) Release consistently — 1 track every 4-6 weeks for the next 3-4 months. Each release triggers Release Radar for your followers, giving the algorithm fresh data. (2) Focus on save rate, not streams. Ask fans to save, not just stream. A saved song tells Spotify the listener wants to hear it again. (3) Target your strongest cohort — don't experiment wildly right now. Release music that matches what originally attracted your listeners. (4) Use Spotify Canvas on every track (increases engagement metrics). (5) Submit for editorial playlists on every release. Even if you don't get picked, the submission metadata helps Spotify categorize you correctly. (6) Leverage your back catalog — create playlists featuring your old hit alongside new music. Share these playlists to drive listeners from old work to new.

The timeline: Algorithmic rebuilding typically takes 3-5 consistent releases (3-6 months). You won't see an overnight turnaround, but by release #3 or #4, the algorithm should start expanding your Discover Weekly placement again. Track your 'Source of Streams' in Spotify for Artists — when algorithmic sources start climbing back above 30%, you're winning.

My distributor keeps asking about ISRCs and metadata. I just want to upload my music — why does metadata matter so much? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Metadata is literally the infrastructure that connects your music to your money. Bad metadata = lost royalties. Here's why every field matters:

ISRC (International Standard Recording Code): A unique 12-character code assigned to each individual recording. Think of it as your track's Social Security number. Every version of a song gets its own ISRC — the album version, the radio edit, and the remix all have different ISRCs. Your distributor usually assigns these automatically. WHY IT MATTERS: ISRCs are how DSPs, PROs, and collection societies match streams to the correct recording. Without a proper ISRC, your plays can be credited to the wrong track or not credited at all.

ISWC (International Standard Musical Work Code): A unique identifier for the composition (the song itself, independent of any specific recording). Assigned by your PRO when you register the work. WHY IT MATTERS: ISWCs link your composition to its publishing royalty chain. Without it, The MLC and international societies struggle to match your song to your publishing account.

UPC/EAN (barcode): Applied to your release (album, EP, single) as a whole. Your distributor assigns this. Essential for physical retail and digital storefront identification.

Critical metadata fields and why they matter: Artist name: must be consistent across ALL releases and ALL platforms. 'DJ Example,' 'Dj Example,' and 'dj example' are three different artist profiles. Genre tags: DSPs use these for algorithmic placement. Tag accurately — don't tag pop if you're ambient. Incorrect genre tags can tank your algorithmic performance. Mood/energy tags: influence which algorithmic playlists you appear on. Song credits: writer names, producer credits, featured artist credits. These link to PRO databases. If credits are wrong, royalties get misrouted. Release date: incorrect dates can disqualify you from New Music Friday and Release Radar. Language: affects which markets your music is promoted in.

Common metadata mistakes that cost money: Typoing credits (misspelled co-writer name = their PRO can't match their share). Listing the wrong PRO affiliation. Using a different artist name spelling than your PRO registration. Not including featured artist credits properly (ft. vs feat. vs featuring). Releasing without registering compositions with your PRO and The MLC.

Best practice: Create a metadata document (spreadsheet) for every release: track title, ISRC, all writers' full legal names, PRO affiliations, IPI numbers, publishing percentages, producer credits, and featured artist credits. Fill this out BEFORE distribution.

I'm trying to distribute my father's catalog from 1987–1999. He's the original artist and recorded these tracks himself, but there are no written contracts, no recording agreements, and no copyright registrations. A label is now claiming they have a distribution deal. How do I establish chain of title and fight their claim? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":false,"hasImages":false}]

Pre-contract-era catalog requires reconstructing title through secondary evidence. Here's the framework: **Step 1 — Establish authorship.** Copyright vests automatically in the author at creation (17 U.S.C. §102). Your father created these recordings — that's the foundational fact. Gather: (1) Original tape boxes, hard drives, or media with creation dates; (2) Studio receipts or booking invoices in his name; (3) Contemporaneous correspondence (letters, emails if post-1995) referring to the recordings as 'his'; (4) Anyone who witnessed the recording sessions — affidavits from co-musicians, engineers, or producers carry weight. **Step 2 — Register now.** File copyright registration for all works at copyright.gov. You can register as 'sound recording' (master) and 'musical work' (composition) separately. Registration now creates a legal presumption of ownership from the date of creation. **Step 3 — Challenge the label's claim.** Ask the label to produce their distribution agreement — not a distribution agreement, their actual written agreement with your father. Distribution deals do not transfer copyright ownership; they're licenses. Even if a valid distribution agreement exists, it doesn't override his copyright. **Step 4 — The '35-year rule.'** Under 17 U.S.C. §203, works created after January 1, 1978 can have copyright transfers or licenses terminated by the author (or heirs) starting 35 years after the grant. The 1987–1999 catalog is within the Section 203 termination window right now — even if a valid license exists, your family may have the right to terminate it.

I have a song with 3 co-writers: I own 40%, Writer B owns 35%, Writer C owns 25%. Writer B has a co-pub deal at 50/50 with Publisher X. Writer C has an admin deal with Publisher Y at 85/15 (C keeps 85%). Publisher X has a sub-publishing deal in the UK at 75/25 (X keeps 75%). A UK sync generates £20,000 in publishing income. Show me the complete waterfall calculation to the final net for each party. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true,"hasAudio":false,"hasImages":false}]

Complex waterfall — let me trace every deduction. **Starting pool: £20,000 UK sync income.** **Step 1 — Sub-publisher deduction (Publisher X's UK deal at 75/25):** Publisher X's sub-publisher in the UK takes 25% of the total income they're entitled to administer. But first, let's establish shares. Publisher X administers Writer B's 50% publisher share (B's co-pub deal = 50% of B's 50% total share = 25% of total song). So sub-pub deduction applies to £20,000 × 25% = £5,000. Sub-publisher takes 25% = £1,250. Publisher X receives: £5,000 - £1,250 = £3,750. **Step 2 — Co-pub split (Publisher X / Writer B, 50/50):** Publisher X's net £3,750 is split: Publisher X keeps £1,875; Writer B receives £1,875. **Step 3 — Admin deal (Publisher Y / Writer C, 85/15):** Writer C's total share: 25% of £20,000 = £5,000. Publisher Y takes 15% = £750. Writer C nets £4,250. **Step 4 — My share (40%, no deal):** £20,000 × 40% = £8,000. No deductions; I receive full £8,000. **Final net per party:** Me: £8,000; Writer B: £1,875; Publisher X: £1,875; Writer C: £4,250; Publisher Y: £750; UK sub-publisher: £1,250. **Check:** £8,000 + £1,875 + £1,875 + £4,250 + £750 + £1,250 = £20,000 ✅

I'm an independent artist who owns my masters. I want to collect all neighboring rights income I'm entitled to. SoundExchange seems to only pay me as the performer. Walk me through how to ensure I'm collecting both the performer share AND the label share from SoundExchange and international neighboring rights organizations. [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution"}]

SoundExchange dual registration protocol for indie artist/label: SoundExchange divides master royalties into three pools: 50% Featured Artist (the performer on the recording), 45% Rights Holder (whoever owns the master — the label), and 5% Non-Featured Musicians (session players/background vocalists). If you own your masters AND performed on them, you're entitled to BOTH the 50% featured artist pool AND the 45% rights holder pool = 95% of total SoundExchange royalties. How to claim both: (1) Register as a Featured Artist: SoundExchange.com → Create account → Artist registration. Associate your legal name with your stage name. (2) Register as a Rights Holder / Label: This is a SEPARATE account under your label name. SoundExchange requires a label account for the 45% rights holder share. Register your label entity at SoundExchange and link all your ISRCs to your label account. Many indie artists only register step 1 and permanently miss the 45% rights holder share. International neighboring rights: For your master recordings played on radio and in public venues in the UK, EU, Canada, Australia, etc.: UK: Register with PPL (Phonographic Performance Ltd) as the label AND as the performer (PPL manages both). Germany: GVL (producer side via your label entity). France: SCPP or SPPF (producer side). These require separate registrations or a neighboring rights administrator who does it for you. Symphonic Distribution, Ari's Take ANR, and specialized firms like ANRFACTORY collect international neighboring rights on your behalf for a percentage (typically 20-30% of recovery).

I woke up to find all 47 of my releases taken down from Spotify and Apple Music. My distributor says there was a 'label identity conflict' and they removed my catalog pending review. This happened without warning and I'm losing income every day. What are my options? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Unauthorized catalog takedown — escalate immediately on all fronts simultaneously: Distributor track 1 (immediate): Call your distributor — do not email. Distributors have an emergency line for rights disputes. Ask for: (1) the specific 'label identity conflict' details — who claims to own your label name or catalog?; (2) the takedown request origin — was this generated by another distributor, a label, or an automated system?; (3) a timeline for restoration. If the conflict is a clerical error or automated match against a similarly-named label, this can be resolved in 24-48 hours with documentation of your ownership (formation docs, contract history). DSP track 2 (simultaneous): Contact Spotify through Spotify for Artists 'Get Support' → 'Takedown Dispute.' Apple Music through Apple Music for Artists support. Submit your distributor contract, your label registration documents, and any copyright registration for the affected works. This creates a parallel resolution track. Documentation you need: Certificate of Formation for your label entity, any trademark registration for your label name, your original distribution agreement showing your catalog with ISRCs and UPCs. Income loss: Document the daily income loss (average daily earnings from the past 90 days) — this establishes damages if the takedown was wrongful. You may have recourse against the party that filed the false claim. Legal escalation: If the distributor won't restore within 72 hours, a letter from an entertainment attorney threatening tortious interference damages often accelerates resolution.

My release got rejected by Apple Music with a DDEX error code. The distributor just says 'metadata issue' but won't tell me exactly what's wrong. What are the most common DDEX ERN rejections? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"electronic"}}]

Let me run `run_metadata_qc` on your release to identify the specific issue. In the meantime, here are the most common DDEX ERN 4.x rejections that Apple Music flags:

**1. Missing or duplicate ISRC:** Apple requires a unique ISRC per track. If two tracks share an ISRC, or if a track has no ISRC assigned, the entire release is rejected. Fix: verify each track has a unique, properly formatted ISRC (CC-XXX-YY-NNNNN).

**2. Territory/rights conflict:** The DealsElement in your ERN specifies which territories the release is available in. If you claim worldwide rights but have an existing release of the same ISRC in a different territory through another distributor, Apple flags a conflict. Fix: ensure you don't have overlapping territorial claims.

**3. Audio file specification mismatch:** Apple requires minimum 16-bit/44.1kHz WAV or FLAC. If the declared audio spec in the ERN doesn't match the actual file (e.g., ERN says 24-bit but file is 16-bit), rejection. Fix: make sure declared specs match actual file properties.

**4. Artwork dimension non-compliance:** Apple requires exactly 3000×3000px minimum, RGB color space, JPG or PNG. Square. If your artwork is 2999px, it gets rejected.

**5. Release date in the past:** If the DealsElement specifies a release date that's already passed and Apple didn't receive the file before that date, they may reject. Fix: update the release date to at least 5 days in the future.

**6. Explicit content mismatch:** If ParentalWarningType says 'NotExplicit' but Apple's content scanner flags explicit lyrics, rejection.

The fact that your distributor won't tell you the specific error is a red flag about their support quality. Ask them for the exact DDEX error response XML — they receive it from Apple.

How often should I be releasing music? Some people say release a single every month, others say save it all for an album. What actually works in 2026? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

The streaming era has fundamentally changed release strategy. Here's what the data shows in 2026:

The Case for Singles (Every 4-6 Weeks): Spotify's algorithm rewards consistency. Each new release triggers Release Radar for your followers. More releases = more algorithmic entry points for new listeners. You can test which songs resonate before committing to an album sequence. Marketing effort is more manageable per release.

The Case for Albums/EPs (Every 6-12 Months): Albums create cultural moments and deeper fan engagement. Higher per-release press coverage potential. Physical product (vinyl, CD) revenue requires a full-length project. Albums are still the format that labels, managers, and booking agents evaluate.

The 2026 Hybrid Strategy (Most Effective): Release 3-4 singles over 4-5 months, building momentum. Then release the album/EP containing those singles plus new tracks. This is called the 'waterfall' strategy — each single pre-released adds streams to the album's first-week count.

Example timeline: Month 1: Single 1 (lead single, strongest song). Month 3: Single 2 (with music video). Month 4: Single 3 (featuring a collaborator for cross-pollination). Month 5: Full album drops, containing all 3 singles + 5-7 new tracks. Month 6-8: Release 1-2 deluxe tracks, remixes, or acoustic versions to extend the album cycle.

Key rules: Never release on the same day as a major artist in your genre (check Spotify's New Music Friday in advance). Fridays are the global release day. Don't go more than 8 weeks without releasing something (single, remix, feature, live version). Keep at least 4 weeks between releases for playlist pitching windows.

At minimum, aim for 6-8 releases per year (mix of singles, features, and one larger project). Quality always beats quantity — don't release filler just to maintain cadence.

My Aspera delivery to Spotify's DDEX endpoint works, but the same config fails for Apple Music with 'SSH authentication failed.' I'm using the same private key for both. What's different about Apple's credential setup? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

SSH authentication failure on Apple Music's Aspera endpoint while Spotify works almost always means one of three credential-chain issues specific to Apple's configuration:

**Issue 1 — Apple uses certificate-based SSH, not raw key:**
Apple Music's Aspera endpoint may require an SSH certificate (public key signed by Apple's CA) rather than a standalone RSA/ECDSA key. Spotify typically accepts raw keys. If Apple gave you a signed certificate alongside your key, you must use both:
```bash
ascp -i /path/to/private_key.pem -i /path/to/certificate.pub ...
```
Check: Does your Apple delivery documentation include a `.pub` certificate file (not just the key)?

**Issue 2 — Host key verification mismatch:**
Apple may have rotated their FASP server's host key since you last connected. Check:
```bash
ssh-keyscan -p 33001 [apple-aspera-host] >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts
```
Then retry. If the known_hosts entry is stale, authentication fails before the key exchange.

**Issue 3 — Key format mismatch:**
Apple sometimes requires RSA 2048-bit keys specifically; ECDSA keys (which work on most servers including Spotify) may be rejected. Check which algorithm your key uses:
```bash
ssh-keygen -l -f /path/to/private_key.pem
```
If it shows `ecdsa-sha2-nistp256`, you may need to generate an RSA key specifically for Apple's endpoint and submit the public key to them for authorization.

**Issue 4 — Username mismatch:**
Apple assigns different SSH usernames per account or per delivery type (full catalog vs. incremental). Verify the `-l` username flag in your ascp command matches exactly what Apple provisioned.

**Diagnosis sequence:**
1. Test with verbose SSH: `ssh -vvv -i /path/to/key -p 33001 user@apple-aspera-host` — the verbose output shows exactly where authentication fails
2. Check Apple's delivery spec for current key requirements
3. If certificate-based: submit your public key to Apple's portal and await the signed certificate before retrying

What distribution advantages do I get with a label that I can't get independently? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"label-deal","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

Labels offer genuine distribution advantages — but understand what you're trading for them:

**What Labels Provide:**

| Advantage | Label | Independent |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| DSP relationships | Direct, named contacts at Spotify/Apple | Self-serve editorial submission |
| Editorial playlist access | Higher consideration via rep | Competitive submission |
| Marketing support at DSPs | Co-funded campaigns | Self-funded or none |
| Physical distribution | Global retail (Target, Amazon, etc.) | Limited or POD |
| International reach | Local teams in major markets | Single global distributor |
| Advance funding | Yes (recoupable) | Self-funded |
| Data access | Enhanced analytics, real-time | Standard Spotify for Artists |

**What You Trade:**

| Trade-off | Typical Terms |
|-----------|---------------|
| Master ownership | Label owns for 7-15+ years (or perpetuity) |
| Revenue share | You keep 15-25% (major), 30-50% (indie label) |
| Creative control | Label may have approval rights |
| Release schedule | Label decides when/how you release |
| Exclusivity | Can't release music elsewhere during term |

**The Break-Even Analysis:**
A label deal makes financial sense when the label's distribution advantages generate MORE revenue than you'd earn keeping 100% as an independent.

Example:
- Independent: 100% of $50,000 = $50,000
- Label deal (20% artist share): 20% of $300,000 = $60,000 ✅
- Label deal (20% artist share): 20% of $200,000 = $40,000 ❌

The question isn't 'is a label good or bad?' — it's 'does their amplification MORE than compensate for the reduced percentage?'

What's the difference between DistroKid, TuneCore, CD Baby, and AWAL? Which should I use? [Context: {"currentModule":"agent","hasProject":true,"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

Your distributor choice significantly impacts your revenue, control, and access to platform features. Here's the objective breakdown:

**Distributor Comparison (2026):**

| Feature | DistroKid | TuneCore | CD Baby | AWAL |
|---------|----------|---------|--------|------|
| Pricing model | $22.99/year (unlimited) | $14.99-$49.99/year (unlimited, subscription) | $9.95/single (one-time, part of Downtown Music), $29.95/album (one-time, now part of Downtown Music) | Free (selective) |
| Royalty rate | 100% to artist | 100% to artist | 91% after first year | 80-85% to artist |
| Speed to stores | 1-5 days | 1-5 days | 2-5 days | 2-4 weeks |
| Spotify for Artists | Yes (via OAC) | Yes | Yes | Yes (enhanced) |
| YouTube Content ID | Add-on ($4.99/yr) | Included | Included | Included |
| Publishing admin | No | TuneCore Publishing ($75/yr) | CD Baby Pro ($69) | Built-in |
| Sync licensing | No | No | CD Baby Sync | AWAL Sync |
| Label services | No | Basic | Basic | Yes (marketing, playlisting) |
| Catalog removal risk | If subscription lapses | If annual fee not paid | Never (one-time fee) | At AWAL's discretion |

**My Recommendations:**

*Best for most independent artists:*
→ **DistroKid** — lowest cost for prolific releasers, fastest delivery, unlimited uploads

*Best for catalog protection:*
→ **CD Baby** — one-time fee means your music stays up forever regardless of payment

*Best for career acceleration:*
→ **AWAL** — free distribution BUT selective (they choose who to accept). If accepted, you get label-level support

*Best for full-service:*
→ **TuneCore** — good publishing admin addon, established infrastructure

**The Smart Strategy:**
Use DistroKid for your primary releases, but register with CD Baby Pro or Songtrust for publishing administration. This gives you the best of both worlds: cheap/fast distribution + comprehensive royalty collection.

My distributor is offering me an exclusive distribution deal with a higher commission but better editorial support. Should I go exclusive? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"goal":"fully-independent","stage":"developing","genre":"hip-hop"}}]

As someone committed to staying independent, exclusivity with a distributor should make you uncomfortable by default. Here's why:

**What you're giving up with exclusivity:**
- The ability to switch distributors if they underperform — you're locked in for the contract term (usually 1-3 years)
- Your catalog may be locked: if you want to leave, your existing releases stay with them for a holdback period (often 6-12 months after termination)
- Pricing power: once they have you exclusively, your negotiating leverage for future terms drops

**What they're offering in return:**
- 'Better editorial support' — ask them to define exactly what this means. How many editorial playlist submissions? For which DSPs? What's the success rate? Get numbers, not promises.
- Higher commission to THEM means less money to YOU on every stream. If they're taking 20% instead of 10%, that's a 10% revenue hit across your entire catalog. At 1M streams/month, that's ~$500/month you're giving away.

**The advocate position for an indie artist:** Stay non-exclusive unless the editorial support comes with GUARANTEED placements (not 'we'll pitch you') and the commission increase is marginal (1-3%, not 10%). Your independence is your strategy — don't trade it for a vague promise of playlist support. If their editorial team is actually good, they'll prove it on a non-exclusive trial first.

I sampled a famous song in my track but I don't want to clear it because it's too expensive. Can I just release it anyway? Lots of SoundCloud rappers do it. [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Unknown","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":0,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"distribution"}]

I strongly advise against distributing music with uncleared samples. Here is why.

The Legal Reality:
Using another artist's recording without permission is copyright infringement. Period. It does not matter how common it is on SoundCloud. The consequences escalate with your success:

If Your Song Gets Popular:
1. Content ID Match: YouTube and Spotify will automatically detect the sample through audio fingerprinting (Content ID, Audible Magic). Your track may be taken down or all revenue diverted to the original rights holder.

2. DMCA Takedown: The original rights holder can issue a DMCA takedown notice. Your song is removed from all platforms within 24 hours. Multiple takedowns can get your ENTIRE catalog removed.

3. Copyright Lawsuit: The original rights holder can sue for statutory damages of $750-$150,000 PER INFRINGEMENT, plus your profits from the infringing track, plus their legal fees.

4. Distributor Termination: Most distributor agreements require you to warrant that you own all rights. Distributing infringing content can void your agreement and get your entire catalog pulled.

The SoundCloud Argument: SoundCloud rappers who release uncleared samples are either: flying under the radar because they are too small for rights holders to notice, getting taken down regularly and re-uploading, or playing a risky game that ends badly when success arrives.

Your Options:
1. Clear the sample: Contact the publisher and master owner. Yes, it can be expensive. But it is the only legal path.
2. Interpolation: Re-record the musical elements yourself (replay the melody, chords). You only need to clear the publishing (composition), not the master. Much cheaper.
3. Replace the sample: Use a royalty-free alternative or create something original inspired by the same vibe.
4. License from a sample library: Platforms like Tracklib offer pre-cleared samples with transparent pricing.

We just got approved for Merlin membership. What ongoing compliance requirements do we need to maintain, and what happens to our rates if we fall out of compliance? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution","hasProject":true}]

Merlin membership comes with ongoing obligations. Here's what you need to maintain:

**Ongoing compliance requirements:**

1. **Independence certification (annual):**
You must certify annually that your label remains independent — not majority-owned by a major label (Universal, Sony, Warner) or their subsidiaries. If you take a label deal or equity investment from a major, Merlin membership terminates automatically.

2. **Annual financial disclosure:**
Submit annual revenue figures for membership fee recalculation. Merlin's sliding fee scale adjusts as your revenue grows. Failure to report triggers a default assessment.

3. **Catalog ownership accuracy:**
You must only register catalog for which you hold the master rights. If an artist leaves your label and you no longer control their masters, you must remove their catalog from your Merlin registration.

4. **Authorized distributor maintenance:**
Your catalog must remain distributed through a Merlin-authorized distributor. If you change distributors to a non-authorized one, Merlin rates don't apply until you move back.

5. **No sub-licensing to non-members:**
You cannot sub-license your Merlin rate benefits to third-party labels or artists not under your direct ownership/contract. Merlin rates apply only to your directly controlled catalog.

**Consequences of non-compliance:**
- First violation: written notice and 60-day cure period
- Continued violation: suspension of Merlin rate premiums (DSPs revert to standard rates retroactively for the non-compliant period)
- Serious violation (e.g., falsely claiming independence): permanent termination and potential claw-back of rate premiums received

**Best practice:** Assign one person as your Merlin compliance contact. Set an annual calendar reminder for the certification and financial disclosure deadlines.

My distributor enrolled my catalog in YouTube Content ID. Now I see claims on thousands of YouTube videos using my music. Some of these are fan videos I want to support, some are commercial use. The settings are: monetize (take revenue share), block (take down the video), or track (watch). How do I make the right decision for each use case? [Context: {"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Content ID policy decision matrix — how to set policies for each video type: Monetize (you take ad revenue from the video): Use for: commercial products using your music (ads, branded content, product videos); YouTube channels with significant subscribers monetizing on your music; gaming channels, reaction videos, compilations generating significant revenue. Revenue split: YouTube pays you approximately 55% of ad revenue generated by the video. This is passive income — your music is working for you. Block (video is removed from YouTube): Use for: direct copyright infringement (music videos that rip your official video); spam/bot channels; content that damages your brand (hate content, NSFW without permission). Risk of blocking: fan creators feel alienated; backlash on social media is common. Reserve blocking for genuine infringement only. Track (monitor only, no action): Use for: fan lyric videos (they're providing value and promotional exposure); genuine fan-made videos from engaged supporters; small channels (<5k subscribers) where monetization revenue is negligible but goodwill is significant. Strategic recommendation: Set your DEFAULT policy to 'Monetize' — this captures revenue from all uses that aren't explicitly blocked. Then manually whitelist specific channels (fan creators you want to support) for 'Track' status. Block only clear infringement. Most independent artists regret blocking fan content because it destroys the organic promotion value. A policy of 'monetize everything, manually whitelist superfans' maximizes both revenue and community goodwill.

How does music distribution work on TikTok? Do I need to do something special to get my song on TikTok? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Luna","genre":"Pop","monthlyListeners":40000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Luna, TikTok distribution is straightforward from a technical standpoint, but optimizing for the platform requires strategy.

Getting Your Music on TikTok:
Most major distributors automatically deliver to TikTok's sound library: DistroKid (auto-included), TuneCore (auto-included), CD Baby (auto-included). When your song is distributed, it appears in TikTok's music library and users can add it to their videos.

TikTok Revenue:
TikTok pays artists when their songs are used in videos. The rates are significantly lower than streaming platforms ($0.001-$0.003 per creation), but the volume can be enormous. Revenue is calculated based on the number of video creations using your sound, not the views those videos get.

Optimizing for TikTok Virality:

1. The 15-Second Hook: TikTok clips default to 15-60 seconds. Your most ear-catching, memorable moment needs to land within the first 15 seconds. Many viral TikTok songs succeed because of one specific 15-second segment.

2. Create the First Videos Yourself: Do not wait for organic discovery. Upload the first 5-10 TikToks using your own music. Establish a 'use case' — what kind of video is this song the soundtrack for? (workout, get-ready-with-me, emotional moment, dance, etc.)

3. Anchor to a Trend or Template: Create a trend or challenge around your song. The easier it is for others to participate, the more likely it spreads.

4. Engaging Caption Strategy: Include your song title and a call to action encouraging duets or stitches.

5. Cross-Platform: Once your song gains traction on TikTok, the listeners migrate to Spotify/Apple to save the full track. This is the TikTok-to-streaming pipeline.

Timeline:
- Upload to your distributor with TikTok delivery selected
- Allow 3-7 days for delivery to TikTok's sound library
- Start creating content IMMEDIATELY once the sound is available
- Double down if you see organic pickup within the first 2 weeks

I know a guy who says he can get my song on Spotify's RapCaviar playlist for $5,000. He says he has connections to the editorial team. Should I do it? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Unknown","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":0,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"distribution"}]

I need to be very direct: this is almost certainly either a scam or illegal payola. Do NOT pay this person.

Why This Is a Scam:
1. Spotify's editorial team does NOT sell playlist placements. Spotify explicitly prohibits third-party payment for editorial placement and has fired editors who engaged in this practice.
2. Nobody outside Spotify's editorial team has the ability to guarantee editorial placement. Even label executives cannot guarantee specific editorial placements.
3. If your $5,000 goes to a fraudulent middleman, you have zero recourse.

Why This Could Be Illegal:
Payola — paying for radio play or playlist placement without disclosure — violates FCC regulations (for radio) and can violate consumer protection laws (for playlists). While streaming payola is a legal gray area, it violates Spotify's Terms of Service and can result in permanent removal.

What Would Actually Happen:
- Scenario A (Most Likely): The person takes your $5,000, does nothing, and disappears.
- Scenario B: They add your song to a bot-filled third-party playlist named similarly to RapCaviar but it is NOT the editorial playlist. The streams are worthless.
- Scenario C (Worst Case): They actually have a corrupt connection, your song goes up, Spotify investigates, your music gets removed from the platform entirely.

Legitimate Ways to Get on RapCaviar:
1. Pitch through Spotify for Artists (free)
2. Build genuine streaming momentum through organic promotion
3. Collaborate with artists already in the editorial ecosystem
4. Work with a legitimate publicist or radio promoter who submits through proper channels

I have a 12-track album ready. Should I release singles first? How do I roll the whole thing out for maximum impact? [Context: {"artistProfile":{"name":"Blake","genre":"Hip-Hop","monthlyListeners":200000,"goal":"fully-independent"},"currentModule":"distribution"}]

Blake, the optimal 2026 album rollout strategy uses singles as stepping stones to build momentum. Here is the playbook.

The Modern Album Rollout (16-20 Week Plan):

Week 1-2: Release Single 1 (The Attention Grabber)
- Your most accessible, playlisting-friendly track
- Full Spotify editorial pitch
- Music video or visualizer
- Purpose: Introduce the album era, reset algorithmic profile

Week 6-8: Release Single 2 (The Deep Cut)
- A fan-favorite type track that rewards your core audience
- Announce album title, release date, and cover art with this single
- Open pre-saves for the full album
- Purpose: Keep momentum, build anticipation

Week 12-14: Release Single 3 (The Title Track / Statement)
- The song that defines the album
- Begin heavy promotional push
- Behind-the-scenes content, tracklist reveal
- Purpose: Maximum pre-album hype

Week 16-20: Album Release
- Release on Friday
- 9 new tracks + 3 previously released singles
- The singles continue streaming AND contribute to first-week album numbers
- First-week streaming push across all platforms

Why Singles-First Works:

1. Algorithmic Advantage: Each single release reactivates Spotify's Release Radar and Discovery Weekly for your profile. 3 singles = 3 algorithmic boost opportunities vs. 1 for an album-only release.

2. Playlist Pitching: You can pitch EACH single to Spotify editorial. Three shots at editorial placement vs. one.

3. Waterfall Strategy: When the album drops, previous singles are already part of the album. Their existing streams count toward the album's total. Listeners who saved singles auto-save the album.

4. Revenue: Singles generate revenue during the rollout period. No dead period where you have nothing out.

5. Content Pipeline: Each single gives you content for social media — music videos, behind-the-scenes, lyric content, fan reactions.

Post-Album Strategies:
- Week 1-2 post-album: Push the album's strongest non-single track as a 'focus track'
- Week 4-6: Release a music video for a deep cut
- Week 8-12: Consider a deluxe edition with 2-3 bonus tracks (restarts algorithmic momentum)
- Week 16+: Remixes or acoustic versions to extend the album cycle

Do NOT release all 12 tracks at once with no lead-up. That is leaving 80% of your promotional opportunities on the table.
